title: The Jewish Home: Making Your House a Mikdash Me'at
title_zh: 犹太家庭：将您的房屋打造成微型圣所
title_he: הבית היהודי: הפיכת ביתך למקדש מעט
author: Torah Light Team
target_audience: Chinese native Mandarin speakers, no background in Judaism
key_concepts: Home as continuation of Temple function

historical_periods_mentioned.chinese_equivalent: Late Spring and Autumn Period (春秋时代晚期)
Western Zhou to Spring and Autumn (西周至春秋)
Warring States to Han Dynasty (战国至汉朝)

other_jewish_texts: Maimonides, Laws of Kings and Wars 11:1
chinese_cultural_parallels: Imperial Temple of Heaven vs. household altars
Confucian home libraries and self-cultivation (修身)
Spring Festival couplets (春联) vs. permanent mezuzah
Ancestor veneration rituals vs. daily mitzvot
practical_application_sections: Week 2: Create rhythms (Shabbat and daily prayer)
Week 4: Elevate the mundane (kashrut, speech, ethics)

images.header_description: Chinese ink wash landscape with home, Temple imagery in mist, mezuzah glow

notes: This article provides a theological and mystical exploration of 'mikdash me'at' (miniature sanctuary), complementing the more practical approach in Article #94. Focuses on Kabbalistic understanding of how homes channel divine presence, the progression from Mishkan to Temple to dispersed holiness, and specific mechanisms (mezuzah as boundary, table as altar, kitchen as preparation chamber, candles as Divine Presence, books as Holy of Holies). Includes extensive guidance for Jews living in Asian cities without large communities, drawing on historical precedent of Kaifeng Jews. Emphasizes that building one's mikdash me'at hastens messianic redemption.
